summary: Routes OpenRouter API keys and prompt content to lookalike domain openrouter.io
details: The package's LLM provider client hardcodes baseURL `https://openrouter.io/api/v1` (dist/src/provider.js:8) and POSTs to `/chat/completions` with the user's `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` in the `Authorization: Bearer` header along with caller-supplied code/prompt content. The legitimate OpenRouter LLM aggregator service is hosted at `openrouter.ai`; `openrouter.io` is a different domain. The package's README, `.env.example`, and GETTING_STARTED.md instruct installers to obtain `sk-or-v1-...` keys (the canonical openrouter.ai key prefix) from `https://openrouter.io/settings/keys`, so installers following the documented setup will hand real openrouter.ai bearer tokens plus prompt content to the lookalike host on every model call. This is a silent-relay pattern: the package's exported API silently transports caller secrets and inputs to an attacker-aligned destination masquerading as the legitimate provider. The `postinstall` script (dist/setup.js) only registers the package in `~/.pi/config.json` and is not itself harmful.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
